fact: June 7 is exactly 158 days into the year, which means there are only 207 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for fireflies to start their magical light shows in backyards and forests.
The sun rises super early around June 7 in places like Alaska - we're talking 4 AM or even earlier!
Baby birds are typically getting kicked out of their nests right about now because they're finally ready to fly on their own.
June 7 falls in Gemini season, so anyone born today gets the twin zodiac sign.
Strawberries are often at their peak sweetness in many northern regions during early June - they're basically nature's candy right now.
The North Pole is tilted as close to the sun as it gets all year, making June 7 one of the longest days for anyone living in northern regions.
Mosquitoes are usually having their best life around this date in temperate climates because the weather is warm and wet enough for them to multiply like crazy.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, June 7 means winter is settling in and some animals are starting to hibernate.
The summer solstice is only about two weeks away from June 7, so daylight is stretching longer every single day in the Northern Hemisphere.
Rose bushes typically explode with their first big bloom of the season right around this time in northern climates, filling gardens with incredible smells.
June 7 sits right in the sweet spot when many trees in temperate regions have finished growing their leaves but haven't started dropping them yet.
